Like the :func:`~jinja-filters.sort` filter, sorting and grouping is case-insensitive by default. The ``key`` for each group will use the case of the first item in that group. Pass ``case_sensitive=true`` to opt back into case-sensitive sorting and grouping. You can pass ``with_counts=True`` to also get the number of items in each group, without having to call ``|list|length`` on the group's items. ``groupby`` then yields namedtuples of ``(grouper, list, count)``. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items, count in users|groupby("city", with_counts=True) %}

""" expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) return _group_by(value, expr, case_sensitive, with_counts) @async_variant(sync_do_groupby) # type: ignore async def do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, case_sensitive: bool = False, with_counts: bool = False, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) return _group_by(await auto_to_list(value), expr, case_sensitive, with_counts) def _group_by( value: "t.Iterable[V]", expr: t.Callable[[t.Any], t.Any], case_sensitive: bool, with_counts: bool, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": sort_key = expr if case_sensitive else (lambda item: ignore_case(expr(item))) out = [] for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=sort_key), sort_key): values = list(values) if not case_sensitive: # Preserve the case of the first item's attribute in this # group, rather than the case-normalized sort key. key = expr(values[0]) if with_counts: out.append(_GroupTupleWithCount(key, values, len(values))) else: out.append(_GroupTuple(key, values)) return out
